---
title: "Data Preparation"
---

# Data Preparation {#sec-data-prep}

`sconjoint` expects conjoint data in *long format*: one row per
(respondent, task, profile). Each forced-choice task contributes
exactly two rows; the binary `choice` column is `1` on the profile
the respondent selected and `0` on the other. Attribute columns hold
the categorical levels or numeric values that were shown on each
profile, and respondent-level moderators (the covariates entering the
DNN's mapping $\mathbf Z \mapsto \boldsymbol\beta$) are repeated
within respondent.

The package does not reshape wide-format data automatically; users
who export from Qualtrics or similar platforms should pivot to long
form themselves with `tidyr::pivot_longer()` or base `reshape()`
before calling `scfit()`. A helper `sc_to_long()` that handles common
patterns will ship in v0.2.

## Reference categories

Each factor attribute is dummy-encoded against a reference level. By
default the reference level is the first level of the factor (or the
alphabetically-first level if the column arrives as character); users
who care about the choice can call `relevel()` directly on the column
before passing the data frame to `scfit()`. The reference level is
absorbed into the intercept of the utility index and does not appear
in the output as its own dummy column.

## Minimum columns

At minimum your data frame must contain:

* a binary 0/1 **choice** column,
* the **respondent**, **task**, and within-task **profile** id columns,
* the conjoint **attribute** columns named on the LHS of the `|` in
  the formula interface,
* the **respondent-level covariate** columns named on the RHS of the
  `|` (at least one Z column is required — the DML estimator is
  defined in terms of $\hat\beta(\mathbf Z)$ and degenerates without
  any moderators).

See @sec-fitting for how these columns are wired into `scfit()`.
